guiding: The poem's very first word is anger. As you read, watch what actually provokes Achilles and what he believes he is owed — what is the quarrel really about, beneath the argument over a captive?
Notice how honour is measured in this world. What concrete things stand in for a warrior's worth, and what is at stake when one of them is taken away?
Watch the moments a god steps into a human decision. When Minerva or Apollo acts on a person, does it replace that person's own reasoning, or give it shape?

factual.prompt: Why does Apollo send a plague on the Achaean camp?
options: Agamemnon dishonoured Chryses, a priest of Apollo, by refusing to ransom his daughter Chryseis — and Chryses prayed to the god for vengeance.
Achilles neglected to sacrifice to Apollo before battle, and the god struck the camp in anger.
The Achaeans plundered Apollo's temple at Troy and carried off its treasures.
Agamemnon broke an oath he had sworn to Apollo to lift the siege within nine years.
factual.explanation: Chryses comes to the ships with ransom; Agamemnon drives him off with threats, so the priest appeals to Apollo, who comes down from Olympus with his bow to strike the camp.

factual.prompt: To compensate for giving up Chryseis, what does Agamemnon take from Achilles?
options: Briseis, the captive woman awarded to Achilles as his prize of honour.
Achilles' armour and his war-horses.
Achilles' command over the Myrmidons.
A double share of the spoils from the next battle.
factual.explanation: The seizure of Briseis — a thing standing for Achilles' honour, not merely a person — is what triggers his withdrawal and the whole plot that follows.

factual.prompt: Who reveals the cause of the plague, and what does he require before naming it?
options: Calchas the seer, who first asks Achilles for protection, since naming the cause will enrage a powerful man.
Nestor, the eldest chieftain, who alone may speak without fear.
Achilles himself, after reading the flight of birds.
Chryses, who returns to the camp to denounce Agamemnon.
factual.explanation: Calchas fears Agamemnon's wrath and secures Achilles' pledge of safety before he will speak — the first sign of the rift to come.

factual.prompt: When Achilles' hand goes to his sword, who checks him — and at whose sending?
options: Minerva, sent by Juno, who loves both men and will not see one kill the other.
Nestor, stepping between them to plead for peace.
Apollo, still angry and unwilling to see more Achaeans die.
Patroclus, seizing his friend's arm.
factual.explanation: Only Achilles sees Minerva; she promises that holding back now will one day win him gifts threefold.

factual.prompt: What does Achilles ask his mother Thetis to do, and how does Jove answer?
options: To beg Jove to favour the Trojans so the Achaeans feel his absence — and Jove bows his head in assent.
To bring him new armour forged by Vulcan so he may fight alone.
To carry him from Troy and end his part in the war.
To make Agamemnon return Briseis at once, through Juno.
factual.explanation: Achilles wants his own side to suffer for dishonouring him — his wrath now turns against the Achaeans themselves.

open.prompt: When Minerva checks Achilles' hand as he reaches for his sword, only he can see her. Is Homer showing a god's intervention, or dramatizing Achilles' own restraint — and does the text let us decide?
Agamemnon commands the greater army; Achilles claims the greater worth. On what does each man rest his claim, and does Book I suggest either is in the right?
Chryses turns to Apollo, Achilles turns to Thetis — two wronged men appeal to a divine parent or patron. What does that parallel imply about how things actually get done in this world?
Homer ends a book of human suffering with Jove and Juno quarrelling and the gods dissolving into laughter on Olympus. Why close on the gods — and why on laughter?
Achilles withdraws knowing his absence will cost his own comrades their lives. Is that a justified response to dishonour, or has his anger outgrown its cause?
